definition: Compute every required train-eval pair exactly, but stream the computation in chunks and write a sparse or long pairwise file instead of holding the full matrix in memory. This is the calibration baseline for SPECTRA audits and for measuring recall of approximate methods.

scale_profile.best_for: Exact train-eval similarity when the number of pairs is tractable or when a gold-standard subset is needed.
scale_profile.typical_upper_bound: Millions to low hundreds of millions of pairs, depending on the cost of the pairwise function and available compute.
scale_profile.avoid_when: The pairwise function is expensive and the train-eval product is too large for scheduled chunked jobs.

required_inputs: pairwise similarity function or vector features

tunable_parameters.name: chunk_size
tunable_parameters.purpose: Controls memory footprint for score blocks.

tunable_parameters.name: top_k_or_threshold
tunable_parameters.purpose: Optionally drops unneeded low-similarity edges while preserving the SPECTRA audit axis.

quality_gates: Verify that chunked results match direct computation on a small sample.
Report whether all pairs, top-k edges, or thresholded edges were retained.
Ensure every evaluation unit has enough train-neighbor coverage for the planned spectral bins.
failure_modes: All-pairs work may still be infeasible for very large datasets.
Top-k truncation can distort low-overlap tails if k is too small.
A non-vectorized pairwise function can dominate runtime even with chunking.
example_usage: python dense_vector_topk_similarity.py --train train.csv --eval eval.csv --out pairwise_similarity.csv --vector-prefix emb_ --metric cosine --top-k 50

citations.title: ANN-Benchmarks: A Benchmarking Tool for Approximate Nearest Neighbor Algorithms
citations.evidence: The benchmark compares approximate methods against exact nearest-neighbor ground truth and reports quality/performance tradeoffs.

citations.title: The Faiss library
citations.evidence: Faiss describes batched vector search, k-nearest-neighbor search, range search, and CPU/GPU execution.
